turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument first this morning in Case 24-557, Villarreal versus Texas. Mr. Banner.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court:  During an overnight recess, the defendant and his counsel have a lot that they need to talk about. They need to go over the testimony that took place that day. They need to prepare for the testimony that's going to be given the next day. These are basic discussions that any competent lawyer would have with a client. This is the assistance of counsel that the Sixth Amendment guarantees. But the defendant and counsel can't have these conversations if they're not allowed to discuss the defendant's testimony. For example, if the defendant's testimony has gone poorly, counsel may need to advise the defendant to accept a plea agreement, but counsel can't do that without discussing the defendant's testimony. The defendant needs  advice about how to testify without violating the trial court's evidentiary rulings, but  counsel can't give this necessary advice without discussing the defendant's testimony. Counsel has an obligation to prevent the defendant from committing perjury, but that would be impossible without discussing the  defendant's testimony. Our brief has many more examples, but the point is that the defendant and counsel often must discuss the defendant's testimony during an overnight recess. Now the court below tried to distinguish between discussions of trial strategy, which it allowed, and discussions of testimony, which it prohibited. But that's no line at all. It's often impossible to discuss trial strategy without discussing testimony, and responsible defense lawyers, worried about being held in contempt for crossing this invisible line, will be chilled from offering the assistance that the defendant needs and that the Sixth Amendment guarantees. The only conceivable rationale for restricting overnight discussion between defendant and counsel is to prevent impermissible coaching. But, as the Court  explained in Geders, there are other ways to prevent coaching. There's no need to prohibit the defendant and counsel from discussing the  defendant's testimony. I invite the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: In the judge's instructions, he says:  I don't want you discussing what you couldn't discuss with him if he was on the stand in front of the jury. What's wrong with that?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Because, if he was on the stand in front of the jury, he wouldn't be -- they wouldn't be allowed to discuss  --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: No, he's saying  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- in-court testimony.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: -- I don't want you discussing anything that you couldn't discuss involving his testimony before it, the jury. That's the standard for the  -- what's permitted and not permitted at  --that evening.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay. Well, the -- the  -- the trial court, if you read -- if you read the entirety of the trial -- the colloquy between the trial court and defense counsel, it's  --it's clear enough that the -- the trial court prohibited all discussion of testimon y, the defendant's testimony, during the overnight recess, and that is how the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals interpreted it. So the  -- the Court of Criminal Appeals  -- the Court of Criminal Appeals interpreted the -- the -- let me get the word  --exact wording exactly right from the  -- from the court's opinion. The court  -- the Court of Criminal Appeals said -- they described the question presented as:  Does a trial judge's sua sponte order that defense counsel could confer with defendant on everything except his ongoing testimony violate the defendant's Sixth Amendment right to counsel?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So would it violate the defendant's Sixth Amendment  right to counsel if the -- during trial he was precluded from being coached or managed by the  -- his attorney?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: You mean during an overnight recess to talk?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: No. During trial.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Oh, during trial. No. During trial --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: What's the difference?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Under  --under Perry -- well, Perry drew a sharp line between overnight recesses and brief daytime recesses. The -- the  -- and the Court said that during a brief daytime recess, like  --like I think you're talking about here  --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: No, what I'm talking about is why is the standard different between what a lawyer can coach or manage with respect to testimony while he's on the stand, which is basically a concern, and what he can coach or manage during the recess.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, no. It's  -- it's the same. The  -- the -- the  --the distinction -- let's be clear about the distinction between impermissible coaching and legitimate, necessary counseling, right, because that's a sharp line. And in answer to your question, that line is the same at all times during the proceeding; that is, whatever is impermissible  before trial begins is also impermissible during an overnight recess. It's impermissible  at any time. And let's talk about what that difference is, right?  So the -- the impermissible coaching is where a  -- the --the lawyer tries to get a witness, the defendant or any other witness, to testify  -- to -- where the lawyer tries to change the substance of the witness's testimony to try to get the witness to testify to something other than that which the witness believes to be true. The court  -- but contrast that with conventional counseling, which is talking about the  -- the testimony in all other contexts, so, for example, advising how to comply with evidentiary rulings. Even  -- even -- even rehearsing the questions and the answers ahead of time.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: But did any of that happen here?  Do we have evidence that any of that happened?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: But it couldn't have happened, it didn't happen, because the trial court barred the defense counsel from discussing Mr. Villarreal's testimony with him during the overnight recess.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Can I follow up on  --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, if you  -- one of the things you say could protect against any problems with your approach --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: -- I'm looking at page 14 of your brief  --you say that the prosecutor could cross -examine the defendant after -- after the --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: -- when the trial picks up again the next day as to the extent of any coaching. Isn't that a real problem with respect to the attorney-client privilege?  What is the prosecutor going to say?  Okay, you had a break, you spent the evening with counsel, what did you talk about?  Objection, Your Honor, attorney-client -- you see how -- I don't see how that could a reasonable counterweight to the problems.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I understand what you're saying. The -- the -- the -- the attorney-client privilege has never been understood to insulate a defendant or any witness from being cross-examined about the extent of impermissible coaching. So the -- the  -- the -- the dialogue would go:  Well, didn't your lawyer tell you to say that?  No, he didn't, or yes, he did --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, what did  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- or something like that.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, what -- what did he tell you to say?  You say, didn't your lawyer tell you to say that or whatever? And he says no. He said, well, what did he tell you to  say or what did he tell you to change?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: The -- the Court in Geders said  -- and we're -- we're merely following that -- that the way -- the way to deter impermissible coaching is for the prosecutor to cross-examine the defendant about it. Now you're right -- you're right that  --to say that functionally it seems like it might raise the same -- same sorts of privilege problems as having the court ask it, but that's what the Court said in Geders and  not just Geders, subsequent cases as well, that the appropriate -- two appropriate ways to deter impermissible coaching. One is  cross-examination by -- by opposing counsel, and the other is just for the trial court to manage the -- the -- the schedule appropriately, do some -- you know, give some foresight to how long the -- the defendant's testimony is going to last, and if the -- if the court is worried about impermissible coaching, just delay the overnight recess some.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But, counsel, I guess I don't understand why you're so focused on impermissible coaching as you have defined it. You say here and you say in your briefs that impermissible coaching is where the lawyer is trying to get the witness to change his testimony or whatnot and that was impermissible both before and after and during --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: All that's fine.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: That is not what I understood to be the concern here. Going back  to Justice Thomas's point, the management of testimony, the talking with the witness about his answers and what some people would call coaching, prepping your witness, can occur  beforehand, right?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right. That's right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But it can't occur while the witness is on the stand. So  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: It --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- Justice Thomas, I think, is pointing to a critical point, which is, to the extent that the lawyer couldn't manage, coach, prep, practice with the lawyer -- with the witness while he's on the stand, why should he be allowed to do so during an overnight recess?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: This  -- this is straight out of Perry, straight out of Perry. So the Court -- the Court in Perry said that during a  -- a brief daytime recess, the -- the -- the court -- the  --the trial court can prevent all contact between the defendant and counsel.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I'm focusing on the content of the crime.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah. No, I understand. I understand.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: But the -- but the -- I'm  -- I'm --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So Perry doesn't help us with that because --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No. Yes, it does because I'm get  -- I'm getting to that, right?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: So the -- the Court said that  --after explaining all of that for a brief daytime recess, the Court then, in the  -- in the next section, in Section -- Section 3 of  --of Perry, the Court says:  Well, overnight recesses are completely different. The Court says:  Admittedly, the line between the facts of Geders  -- that's an overnight recess -- and the facts of this case is a thin one. It is, however, a line of constitutional dimension. The Court goes on to say:  During an overnight recess, it is the defendant's right to  unrestricted -- unrestricted access to his lawyer for advice on a variety of trial -related matters that's controlling. And then here's the key sentence:  The fact that such discussions will inevitably  include some consideration of the defendant's ongoing testimony does not compromise that basic right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right. So that's  -- that's precisely what I'm getting at.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I think there might be  -- and I want you to help me with this --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- a difference between discussions that take into account the testimony, maybe even the fact of the testimony, and something that one could call managing  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- or prepping --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- or, you know, the kind of thing that you have even admitted a lawyer does  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- legitimately to help his client before he takes the stand.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And what I  understood the trial court here to be doing was just eliminating that very narrow category of conduct that a lawyer engages in to actually prepare his witness with respect to particular questions and answers on something.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: And that  -- and that's the line the Court of Criminal Appeals tried to draw, right, between discussion of  -- what you're calling prepping the testimony and discussion of testimony in other contexts.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And why are they wrong about that?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Because that line just  -- I mean, to even call it a line is wrong. It's not a line. It's a Rorschach blot, right?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But that's the line that Perry drew. I mean, if you go up a few sentences from the sentence that you read, the Court says, you know, an overnight witness -- an overnight recess is different. Why is it different?  Because an overnight recess will go to matters that go beyond the content of the  defendant's own testimony, matters that the defendant does have a constitutional right to  discuss with his lawyer, suggesting that as to only the defendant's own testimony, the defendant does not have a constitutional right to discuss with his lawyer. Now then it talks about how, of course, when you talk about the protected matters, there might be some incidental discussion of the testimony itself. So it concedes that perfectly willingly. But it draws a pretty sharp line between matters going to trial strategy and matters going to trial testimony of the defendant itself and says that that's the reason why the recess -- the overnight recess versus 15 -minute recess makes a difference.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: And, like I said, that's no line at all. And the way you can  --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But that's Perry's line. I mean --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: But, no, I don't -- no, I  --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- it might -- it might be that Perry was wrong, but that's Perry's line.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, I  --I -- I disagree. Perry -- Perry  --again, Perry -- Perry goes on to say that a defendant has an unrestricted right of access to his lawyer during an overnight recess, including for consideration of the defendant's testimony. So let me  --let me g et -- so you're -- you're drawing -- you're trying to draw the same line that the Court of Criminal Appeals drew.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah, I'm not trying to draw it. I'm suggesting that Perry --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- says it right here  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- on page 284. (Laughter.)

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That's  --that that's the key language from Perry. Now Perry goes on to say, as I think your friend in Texas goes on to say, that there can be all kinds of places where the testim ony has to be talked about as incidental to what is protected, which is the discussion of trial strategy, but not in and of itself.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay. First of all, I  -- I dis- -- disagree respectfully with -- with your view of what  -- of what Perry held, but if Perry held that, I think it's -- I think it's just incorrect, right?  So let's -- let's work through some examples. So -- so the  -- before --before the overnight recess, in his  --in his testimony, the defendant has come very  -- an unsophisticated defendant has come very close to mentioning excluded evidence that would be  -- that would be absolutely devastating. During the  -- the -- the -- the overnight recess, defense counsel needs to say to the defendant:  Look, you nearly mentioned this  --this  --this evidence. Look, when we resume again tomorrow, you better remember not to mention that because we're -- we're going to be in big trouble if you  --if you -- if you -- if you mention that tomorrow. Okay. Is that  --is that  --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: See, I --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: --consideration or is that discussion?  You have to factor into that  --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. -- Mr. Banner, I  -- I -- I think maybe I'm missing something, but I would think that would be permissible because it doesn't refer to the testimony itself. It can simply be a reminder:  Hey, don't -- don't go here, that might implicate --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, but it -- but it does  --it does refer  --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: If I might finish. If I might finish.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Oh, sorry. I'm sorry.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I -- I -- I think what my colleagues are getting at and -- and what I kind of thought you even conceded in your brief is that coaching  -- and maybe we're defining coaching differently, perhaps that's it  -- but that there are some things that a district court can constitutionally prohibit counsel from doing and -- while a witness is on the stand, even if there's a recess. You -- you agree with that, right? You think there are some things that can be prevented?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Absolutely. Coaching, in the sense of suborning perjury, altering the substance of the witness's --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, I'm not talking about suborning perjury. I'm talking about coaching.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, but  --but -- but we  --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: All right. If you don't like that word, let's use management, okay, which is the word Texas used.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I like that word even less.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Why  -- why -- what  --what in the Constitution, what in history suggests that you have a right to manage a witness's testimony during a break --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- as opposed to derivative or collateral matters that you do  need to advise him on?  There's a plea agreement, and the way things have gone today, maybe we ought to take that plea. There's some excluded evidence, be careful not to step into that. I -- I can see all of those kinds of comments, but I'm  -- I'm having a hard time understanding historically, traditionally, what have you got that says that there's a right of a witness to  --to be coached or, if you don't like that word, managed by his attorney while he's not on the stand?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Whatever the length of time.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah. So let's  --let's talk about the -- the --the history. So the  -- the precise question presented in this case could not have arisen at the founding because defendants weren't allowed to testify until the late --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Obviously.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: --19th century. Right. And so the question is, what's the appropriate inference to be drawn from historical practice, right?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And  -- and -- and historical practice is that once you become a witness, you  --you are generally subject to the rule that you can't talk about your testimony, and that's generally understood to mean coaching and  managing but not advice about other legal matters. That  -- that's -- that's my understanding of the traditional rule.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay. I've got  --I've got three points I'd like to make about the history.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Please.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay. First of all, it's crystal-clear at the  --at the founding and all  -- all the way until now that while the defendant -- there are times when a defendant can certainly be denied access to counsel. When he does have access to counsel, he has a right to whatever assistance, including discussion of testimony, will be -- will be -- will be useful to him, right?  So the  -- there's -- there's  -- there's  -- while there's certainly historical warrant, as the government points out, both -- both governments point out, there's certainly historical warrant for saying that there are times when a defendant lacks access to counsel. So, you know, in a prison in the middle of the night or something like that, sure. But there's no historical warrant for saying that when a defendant does have access to counsel, the trial court can say:  Well, yo u can talk about Topic A, but you can't talk about Topic B.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Would your rule apply to witnesses who have counsel?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No. No, no, no, because non-party witnesses, they don't have a Sixth Amendment right that a -- that a defendant has.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: This is a rule specific to the defendant.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, they have -- they have a right to counsel and they brought counsel. I  mean, would -- would --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: During a criminal -- a right to counsel during  --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Would it apply as well in civil proceedings too --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No. No, no, no, no.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- to the defendant there?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, no, no, no, no. The trial court can sequester non-party witnesses. This case isn't about that. This case is about -- about the defendant as a witness.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: In civil  -- in civil proceedings, where there's a right to counsel, presumably?  Yeah, it would apply there, I think, your rule.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I  --I don't know. I mean, the -- the -- the --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: It's just that there's a long historical tradition of witnesses going on the stand being told something like what the trial court said here.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Non -- non-defendant witnesses.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So, Mr. Banner, can I just clarify?  You're entirely rejecting the line that Justice Kagan drew to your attention, and you're saying that even during the day, let's say it was an hour  -- you're saying this is all about time -- so, even if the recess was for an hour for lunch, you are saying that the district judge cannot restrict what the counsel and the client, the defendant, can discuss? That's not what you're saying?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, no, no, no. No, no, no. So, again, this is  -- this is Perry. Perry says that during a  --a -- a -- a daytime, brief daytime recess, the court can cut off all contact.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: But what if he doesn't cut off all contact?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, and then there's a footnote in  --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So, if -- so you're saying that once the district court allows contact, no restrictions?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, no, no, because  -- there's a footnote in Perry, maybe is what you're leading up to, that -- that -- that basically the greater includes the lesser; that is, if -- if  --where the trial court can cut off access to counsel, the trial court could allow selective access to counsel. But that  -- that logic doesn't apply during an overnight recess, when the court has  held there's an unrestricted right of access to counsel.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So it's all about time?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: It's all about time.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: If it were three hours, if it were four hours, and it was daytime  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, look, the  --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- how do you -- how do you decide when it's too long?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: So -- so, in theory, there could be some hard cases between 15 minutes in Perry and overnight in -- in Geders. I have to say, in practice, the lower courts have had no trouble drawing this line. Daytime recesses are usually pretty short. Overnight recesses are much, much longer. It's very easy to tell  --to  --to  -- to draw that line. Now, in theory, you know, if a court were to say, look, we're going to have a recess from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. and we're all going to  --we're going to be like  a vampire court and just do it at night, you know, maybe  --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. So  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: --you get some hard drawn lines.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: -- your rule then is that anytime during the day when there is a recess, the district court could, subject to maybe some extreme case where it goes on for too long and you have a vampire court, the court could say you can talk to your lawyer about everything, except the lawyer cannot manage your testimony during the day?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Again, because  --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: During the day?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: During the day, again, because the greater includes the lesser.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Quite right.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And you can't  -- but you can't do that at night?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Night, correct, because, at night, during over --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: It's unrestricted because Geders says that. So there's no --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well  --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: You can't have a line at night?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, Geders says that  --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: You can have a line during the day but not at night, right?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah, because Geders says that, but Geders -- Geders was correct in saying that, right?  Geders  --Geders pointed out correctly that overnight recesses are  -- have always been times of intense strategizing, discussion, and so on and -- and whereas Perry said, well, not so for -- for -- for brief daytime recesses. Let me, if I could  --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Mr. Banner, can I ask you  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- a concrete question?  I can read Geders and Perry, but I'd like a con -- an answer to a concrete question. And let me give you an example. Let's suppose that a very important issue in a case is the meaning of Exhibit A, and in pre paring the witness to testify, defense counsel goes over what the witness is going to say about Exhibit A. Doesn't put words in his mouth, but you know how it's done. So it's all  prepared, a way of dealing effectively from the defense standpoint with Witness A -- with Exhibit A. Then, when the witness gets on the stand and is under cross-examination, the witness fall -- you know, the witness departs from that and says things that are quite damaging. Now, during a recess overnight or during the day, well, let's just say overnight, can defense counsel talk to the witness about that and say, look, you know, the understanding was you were going to say this, but you said something different, this is very damaging, that's allowed?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, sure, the  --yes. The  -- the defense counsel can say, look, we -- when we rehearsed this ahead of time, you were going to say A, B, and C.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right?  But, instead, you've said something totally different, which is D. Now you've got -- you've got to tell me  which of these is actually correct because, if you said D accidentally, then tomorrow we need to correct that in  --in questioning. If -- if you said D --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Okay. The next day  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- the next day, when the defendant takes the stand, everything is cleared up. Now he's back to -- you know, it's a sea change  back to what was rehearsed, your word, in your words, before trial. Can -- can the prosecutor on cross-examination say, well, you said this yesterday, now you say this today, did you talk to your lawyer last night?  Yes, I did. How long did you talk to your lawyer?  Did you talk about this?  Can he ask whether they talked about that issue?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well  --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Is that a violation of the attorney-client privilege?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- I don't -- I don't think so. I don't think so because the -- as the Court -- as the Court's held several times, the  -- the -- the -- the proper way to ferret out any impermissible coaching is cross-examination and then the prosecutor arguing to the jury, look, this -- this witness is not credible because this witness was -- was  -- was told what to say.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I mean, that's very interesting because I thought the core of the attorney-client privilege had to do with communications between the attorney and the client about important matters. But you think that  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- the prosecutor --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well  --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- can go into that?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, I mean, if -- honestly, I'm honestly not sure. And maybe -- maybe you're right. I honestly don't know.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, it matters a lot because you're saying cross-examination is the corrective. All right. Suppose we've got a lunch break, a one-hour break for lunch in two things. Absolutely identical defendants, both  --Exhibit A is important for both. In one, there's a lunch break. In one, there's an overnight break. Can they both  --can -- does the same rule apply in those two situations?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: So -- so you're right to suggest  --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, don't tell me what the cases say. Just tell me why it would make sense to have a different rule in those two instances.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Because -- because -- you're right. Let me -- I was about to do that. You're right to suggest that there are  -- there are times when a well-timed overnight recess can be an advantage to a defendant that another defendant who doesn't get an overnight recess wouldn't have. That's absolutely right. But that is a result that is required by the Sixth Amendment because the alternative would be much, much worse. Th e alt  --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, my question -- I want to get to that. But --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- my question has to do with a lunch break or a 15-minute break during the trial versus an overnight break.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right. That's what I'm  -- that's what I'm saying.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Same rule in the  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Different  --no, different -- different result.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Different rule. Why?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Different results.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, two reasons, right? One is that that's what Perry said. We're just  --we're just repeating the holding of Perry. You want to overrule Perry, I'm not going to complain, but that was the  -- that was the -- the holding of Perry. And  -- but, second, it makes -- it makes sense because, as the Court said in Geders, overnight recesses have always been times of strategizing, discussions. This is  -- this is the time when this -- I mean, this is like prime time for  --in the middle of a trial, is an overnight recess.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah. If the defendant is on the ropes during cross-examination, an overnight recess can be very  beneficial.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Very beneficial.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So, once again, two  -- I'll continue this.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, can I -- can I just finish the answer?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, I didn't finish -- I didn't get the question out.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: You didn't get  -- okay.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I  -- I'll get to it.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll be back. Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Okay. Once again. Two  -- (Laughter.)

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- two absolutely identical defendants, two trials. Five o'clock  -- the defendant is on the ropes in cross-examination. Five o'clock rolls around. It would be really helpful for this defendant to have a break, you know, and regroup. So the judge says:  Well, you know what, I  -- how much longer is cross-examination going to be?  And the prosecutor says three hours. So the judge says to the defendant:  Well, you know what, we can go on until 8:00, or we can take a -- we can take an overnight break, subject to the restriction that you  can't talk about the substance of the testimony. What happens then?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, your latter alternative would violate the Sixth Amendment. During an overnight recess, you have a right to talk about your testimony with -- with counsel. It's very -- that's super -- super-important.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So a different result in those two situations?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Different  --it's the distinction between a daytime recess and an overnight recess.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Okay. One last thing. Can you give a -- a succinct definition of coaching?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Coaching is what's not allowed. You said it's  -- you can't suborn perjury.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Impermissible coaching is where the lawyer tries to change the substance of the witness's testimony. So you  -- you -- you  -- you said before that the -- in our -- just talking ahead of time,  you said that the light was green. No, you should say that it was red because that's more helpful to your case. That's impermissible coaching. In fact, the Court discussed this -- excuse me -- the Court --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah, you can't  -- the lawyer can't say, okay, well, this is what you should say.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right. Right. But --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: But you think -- but you know  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: But --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- when you're preparing a witness, I mean, you could do the same thing overnight to get the witness to remember what the witness had said before.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, look, the  --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: That's allowed.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- the -- the -- the other side -- so this -- what I just said was impermissible coaching. Conventional counseling is -- is where the lawyer says, okay, I'm going to ask you this question,  what's your answer going to be?  Client -- the witness, rather, gives the -- gives the answer. Lawyer says, look, that's a big jumble, you know?  Why don't you  --why don't you say it like this, without suggest -- suggesting words, without  -- without changing --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: All right. Thank you very much.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Am I correct to say that you say the only order a district court can give overnig ht is some -- basically, the ABA rule, that a lawyer can only be prohibited from telling the defendant to give false testimony, correct?  That's your definition?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, I mean, I would say  -- I would say --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, just answer yes or no.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, okay. Mostly yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Okay. So assume, please, and don't fight me, that I think that Texas's position is more nuanced, and I accept that improper coaching that could be prohibited included working through  --walking through potential questions and answers, telling a defendant to use one word and not another word, what generally is thought of coaching, but Texas, and I think Justice Kagan defined their position, they can discuss incidental effects of testimony, and that would include  -- and pay attention to the list-- plea bargaining, including telling a defendant that they did lousy and they should take the plea; perjury, you lied and you shouldn't; excluded evidence, the one that you were worried about; other witnesses and where they might be located, contact information. What else -- what is missing from that list, assuming the first bucket?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah. So what  -- what's --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You can't coach. You can't give --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- questions and answers. You can't do -- tell them to use a particular word or change a word, even if it's not perjury.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Assume all of that. What's missing from that list?  What other thing is missing?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, that -- that list covers many of the most important things.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I just said  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: But -- but what it doesn't include is what you specifically excluded at the start, which is going over questions and answers.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. So, if I say no to that but say yes to the incidental effect, there's no other topic that you can imagine?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: You know, off the top of my head, I -- I'm sure there are others. I mean, the -- the -- a -- a defendant and counsel have a million --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So wouldn't the definition -- wouldn't the definition that Texas provides, which is the incidental effects of testimony, is okay?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I  --that's no line at all. How -- how -- that's -- how is anyone  supposed to  --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Assuming I believe there is the workability issue, there's no other topic you can think of?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, if you gave me -- if you gave me a little time, I could probably think of more because there's an infinite number of things.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Now, going back to Justice Thomas's question, there's no facts in this record that would suggest that the defendant wanted to talk about plea bargaining or the counsel wanted to talk about a missing witness, wanted to talk about perjury. There's nothing in this record to suggest any of that, correct?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: That's -- that's -- that's correct. But the reason is that defense counsel understood the court to be barring all discussion of testimony, period, and the court confirmed that.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well,  counsel said  --counsel said, Judge, I understand what you're saying, I won't manage the testimony.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, no. There's -- there -- there  --there's more. So  -- so the colloquy here is at pages 4 and 5 of the blue brief, right?  So the -- the  --the court says, I don't  -- don't -- what you can't discuss is  --this is the  -- the --the second-to-last paragraph on page 4  -- the court says the thing that the  --that you can't discuss is -- the very start of that paragraph  --his testimony. And if you look at the  --the -- the top of page 5, defense counsel says:  We aren't going to talk to him about the facts that he testified about. Court says:  All right. Fair enough. Right?  So the -- the -- the  -- the reason why there's no --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: We can read the transcript. Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay, okay.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan?

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Banner, with respect, you have had a little bit of time since Texas's brief, and Texas, as I think you  say in your reply brief, you say:  Well, Texas has narrowed the con- -- the conflict a great deal and does come up with this set of examples of how discussion of trial testimony would be relevant to matters of trial strategy and so could proceed during a recess. And -- and it seems to me, like, unless you can tell me what other things there are like that, that, you know, Texas is basically saying anything that's relevant to larger matters of trial strategy, that should go in one bucket. But, if all you're doing is going over the testimony, he said this, you said this, you might sort of think about adding this, he said this, but you explained it to me a little bit better when we rehearsed this, if -- if -- you know, is that what you're holding out for, to include that as well?  Is that the only --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah. Well --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- thing that stands as a difference between you and Texas right now?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I'm not -- honestly not sure if it's the only thing, but it's a more important thing than you're suggesting because  -- because, you know, it's a common situation. So  --so a defendant says something completely surprising, something different from what counsel thought that the defendant was going to say. Overnight, they need to be able to say, oh, wait a second, when we talked before, you said A, B, and C, but now you're  -- now you're saying D, E, and F. Why is that?  What do  -- what do  -- that's -- that  --that is crucial for the assistance of counsel. It could be necessary to prevent perjury. At the very least, it's --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. But that's covered by Texas's  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Wel l, but that -- but that was what -- what I understood your question to exclude, right?  You need to be able to talk about the substance of his testimony.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I think what I'm suggesting is -- is that the only difference between you and Texas right now is, like, let's  go over the trial testimony and see if we can do it a little bit better.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah. So -- so -- so  -- well, let -- let me put it this way. So --so our  -- our view is that you just have a right to talk  --talk about your testimony, period, right?  Texas's view as I understand it is that the Sixth Amendment protects this list of 11 things you can talk about but not this other list of seven things that you can't talk about.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah, I don't think that that's fair. I think what Texas is saying, and Texas can correct me if I'm wrong, but it's pretty clear, which is that if the -- if the trial testimony comes up because it's relevant to trial strategy decisions, like whether to take a plea bargain, like whether to go find another alibi witness, like what to do about a piece of excluded evidence, et cete ra, et cetera, then you're allowed to talk about it. But it has to be incidental in that way to trial strategy decisions. It can't just be:  Oh, my gosh, you didn't do that very well, could we try to do it better, thanks.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: But that  --that is a trial strategy decision; that -- that is, how  -- how we're going to present our evidence is a fundamental trial strategy decision.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay. That's a fair response.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But I  -- I take that to be the difference.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I think that's right. I think it's a smaller difference than it used to be. That's right.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: All right. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch?  Justice Kavanaugh?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: I'm trying to figure out the logic of a line based on a 15-minute recess versus a lunchtime recess versus a lunchtime plus I have another matter I have to handle, so it's going to be  a two-hour break recess versus an overnight recess.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, you know, the Court in Perry said it's a thin line, but it's a line of constitutional dimension. If you're asking me to describe --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Where does that come from?  And let me --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, yeah. So  --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- where  -- two things. Where does it come from?  And what's the logic?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Because it strikes me as not especially logical.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay. Let's cover  -- let's talk about the logic first, right?  So the  -- the Sixth Amendment guarantees a defendant a right to the assistance of counsel, right, which should mean at the very least that if the government wants to limit the assistance of counsel, the government better have a very good reason for doing so, okay? And as I understand the -- the logic of Perry, it's -- it's -- it's --it's in large part a concern with trial management. If  defendants had the right to confer with counsel during every tiny little recess, you know, one minute, 10 seconds, or whatever, it would just be impossible to run a trial. So there has to be some -- I think this is the  -- the holding of Perry -- there has to be some -- some line where a recess is  -- is  -- is -- is just too short.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And what  --I mean  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: And where  does -- and where does that -- I mean, the opposite part of that rule --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Where does it come from?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- where does it come from?  The opposite  --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: And it all depends on the -- as a question Justice Blackmun asked in the argument in Geders, it depends on the accident of a recess, which is a very  -- and then, you know, the next page said you can't force a recess.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So the whole thing is treating two classes of defendants very differently. And this might be critical to the outcome of the trial. If the trial judge does a recess, you're -- you're golden, or at least a long enough recess.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: An overnight recess.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Overnight recess.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Yeah, yeah.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: A long enough recess overnight. But, if not, Perry says no. And so those defendants are going to be treated much differently, and -- and does that make a lot of sense?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: I think -- I think it does because, as the Court said in Geders, an overnight recess is  -- has traditionally been a time of intense discussion and strategizing. And the shorter you go in a recess, the less true that is, until you get to 15 minutes, and  --and -- and it's probably not true. But I -- I understand Perry as really the  -- the -- again, you -- the government needs a very strong reason to  -- to -- to override the right to the assistance of counsel. And -- and -- and I read Perry as basically being that reason being just the logistics of trial management.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett?  Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So I guess I don't understand your Sixth Amendment argument with respect to the defendant's ability to get that kind of management from his counsel during trial. So is it your position that a defendant has a constitutional right to consult with his attorney about the answers that he's given, you know, turn to the judge and say: I'd like to  -- to have a moment to talk to my counsel while he's testifying?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: No, of course not. There's no right to call a timeout during -- during your testimony. Of course not.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So why not?  Why not?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, a trial  -- a trial could barely go on if -- if a --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: So just pure logistics is what  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Trial logistics. I think so, right?  Whereas the concern with trial logistics is it vanishes during an overnight recess when everyone's got plenty of time.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right. But, when we  --but Perry suggests that when we have a 15-minute recess already planned and it's there, why couldn't  the lawyer talk about this kind of management with his client during that time?  In fact, Perry says that's all that would be expected during that time and that's precisely why we don't allow it. So I  --I just don't understand -- I don't understand your thought that the lawyer has the right when his client is testifying to talk to him about his questions and answers and coach him as to how to better answer the question.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Okay. I'm going to  -- I'm going to quarrel twice with the  --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- the framing of your question. First, he  --he never -- doesn't have a right to coach him in the sense of impermissible coaching. He has a right to give  advice about the wording of his testimony, not the  -- not the right to coach him.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But just not during the trial while he's on the stand, but he can do that in a 15-minute recess and --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: So he can't do it in a 15- --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: That's Perry.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What difference does time make if he has the right to coach him in that way?  What difference does the  -- does the fact  --

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well  --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- that it's 15 minutes versus overnight?  If the Constitution says that a defendant has a right to be counseled with respect to his answers while he's testifying, I don't understand what difference it makes that we have a recess  or not or whatever.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Well, okay. So the -- but it's a distinction between the daytime and an overnight recess.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: That's the distinction the Court drew in Perry. And -- and, like I said, if you want  to ask me to justify that distinction  --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: -- it's -- I think it's  --it's mostly just a matter of trial management because you think about -- if you say you have a right to counsel, to confer with counsel during a 15-minute recess, you can see the obvious questions, five-minute recess, one-minute recess, and so on. And so I think the Court just said, look, at some point, a recess is just too short.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: STUART BANNER
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Warthen.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court:  When a defendant's testimony is paused for a long break, the trial court may tell defense counsel not to either manage the ongoing testimony, as we propose, or not to  discuss the testimony altogether, as the United States proposes. Both rules are supported by this Court's precedents. In Geders versus United States, this Court barred absolute no conferral orders during long breaks, but it never opined on qualified orders. Indeed, Geders himself would have been fine with such an order. In Perry versus Leeke, this Court allowed both absolute and qualified orders during short breaks, emphasizing the importance of untainted cross-examination to uncovering the truth. Perry reconciled its holding with Geders by noting that there is a constitutional difference between discussing ongoing testimony, which is not protected, and discussing other trial -related matters, which is protected. And as long as protected matters can be discussed, the right to counsel is preserved. Moreover, by allowing qualified orders, Perry necessarily recognized that  counsel can indeed navigate such orders during short breaks, and there is no logical reason  why they could not do so during long breaks as well. Accordingly, qualified orders allow  trial courts to balance what the Constitution actually protects with the integrity of trial, and that's exactly what happened here. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: After the night passed and -- and the  -- the --there was no -- you had testimony by Petitioner, was there some objection to the judge's order in -- in the sense that Petitioner said or his lawyer said that they were not allowed to discuss the matters other than testimony?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor. When they came back the next day, the trial court asked them if there was anything to bring up. They never -- they said nothing from the defense, Your -- Your Honor. And they neve r filed a motion for a new trial. And, in fact, not only that, on top of that, my friends on the other side, they say the only way to enforce these orders is to say, well, what did you talk about last night. But  that didn't even happen. The trial court -- the trial court just started his testimony up the next day and everybody went on. It was assumed that they followed the court's order.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. Warthen, I appreciate the subtlety of Texas's position as compared to the Solicitor General's more absolute rule, and I just have a couple of questions about that distinction.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: First, is there any reason why the Court needs to reach the Solicitor General's more absolute rule in this case?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: The only reason you would have to do that, Your Honor, is if you read the order actually given in this case more broadly.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I think that's right. That would be the only reason why we'd have to reach the government's position.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: You agree with that?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay. And then, when  --

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: If you read it the way we read it, it would only be a managing order -- no managing order and not a -- not a  no-testimony order.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And when we come to that, you've got, I think, the relevant language at page 23 in your brief, where the court says that -- well, actually, where we said in Perry that an overnight recess that would encompass matters that go beyond the content of defendant's own testimony, matters that the defendant does have a constitutional right, those are protected. If we understand the trial court's order in this case to be consistent with that standard, is there any reason why we need to address the Solicitor General's proposal?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: No, I don't believe so.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: If you believe it was just a -- if this is a -- a small, like, I guess you can conceptualize like this, if the  --if this is broad, this is a  --a narrower kind of order.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And give me your best reason why that's the case here.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: That this is a narrow order?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Well, so, if you look at page 7A of the appendix, he says:  I don't want you discussing what you couldn't discuss with him if he was on the stand in front of the jury, his testimony. I'm not sure whatever else you would like to talk to him about when he's on the stand, but ask yourself before you talk to him about something, is this something that manages testimony in front of the jury?  And I think what he's saying here is the only thing you'd want to do while he's on the stand testifying is go up there and say you need to slow down, you need to stop shifting, you need to look at the jury in the eye, you need to remember the things that we talked about as far as the things you should say, here are the questions I'm going to ask you next. That's all managing in the way we define it. And then he tells him:  Just ask yourself when you're talking to him tonight, you can talk to him about something  --and even gives him an example of potentially if they go  into a punishment phase -- you can talk to him about something, but, if  -- if -- if you're going to be managing his testimony, that's a -- you can't do that.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: And, as Justice Thomas pointed out, there were no questions or follow -up clarification  --

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- requests from counsel on that.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Exactly. He gave a little bit more clarification on his own after that, but there was no question about, well, can we talk about perjury if that -- if that's maybe a thing, can we talk about a potential plea bargain?  They di dn't ask any of that.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Warthen.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel  --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, let  -- let -- let's say the defendant and the lawyer go into their, you know, wherever for  the evening recess, and the defendant says something like:  Counsel, you remember when we  were preparing for this, we both agreed we should try to get the jury to focus on Fred, and whenever it's reasonable, I should mention  Fred, and I've been doing that, and I notice every time I do that, you know, Juror Number 8 gets a big frown and shakes his head. He doesn't look to me like he likes the idea of talking about Fred at all. So I think that's a bad idea. Now talking about Fred was your idea. Do you still think it's a good idea?  Can the lawyer respond to that question?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: No. They would have to tell them I'm under a court order not to  -- not to answer that.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: So, at that point, he tells the defendant, who's facing a capital sentence, I'm not going to tell you? It's a very simple thing. Don't talk about -- he's not saying particular things, but let's  -- let's stop talking about Fred whenever we can.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes, that would be managing his testimony. That would be coaching and strategizing with him.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel  --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Do you think that the  --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, go ahead.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Do you think that counsel can say, listen, I've been noticing that you've been mumbling and you're also not making eye contact with the questioner, and it would just be a good idea if you'd stop mumbling and made eye contact?  Can the lawyer do that in an overnight recess?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: No. I would consider that to be coaching their testimony. As far as how you present yourself to the jury, I would say that's also the same as the substance of what  --what you're saying.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah. So the -- the  -- the line that you're drawing -- and you draw this on page 14, and I just want to make sure that -- that you're still saying what you're saying on page 14, where you say direct discussion, i.e., testimonial management, that  is, direct discussion of the testimony, right --

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- can be prohibited. So whether it's the Chief Justice's question or my mumbling question, that's direct discussion. But counsel can still discuss a range of issues related to the testimony, including calling additional witnesses, plea bargains, legal objections, court orders, excluded evidence, and the implications of perjury, among others.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So that's still the line that you're drawing?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes. The touchstone is always the lawyer should contemplate for a moment and ask themselves is this going to cause me to manage their testimony. That -- the way we define that is coaching, regrouping, strategizing about the testimony itself, not about other things related to the -- the -- you could strategize all you want about should we still bring in the expert witness,  things like that. But, as far as the -- how the testimony is ongoing, that would not be allowed.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And when Mr. Banner says that's not a line at all --

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- what is your response to that?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: I think that's a very real line because, I mean, one, the case law draws that line. And I think the logic of Perry is very strong. They say it's an empirical predicate of our system of justice that an uncounseled witness is more likely to tell the truth than one who has time to pause and consult with their attorney. And they  --they say this -- this r ule applies for both witnesses generally and defense  --defendant witnesses. They don't draw a distinction between the two. And I think there's a lot of logic to that because, if it wasn't logical, then we would have to get rid of this rule for all witnesses. I mean, all witnesses would have to be  --let's say this  --we flip it around. Let's just say it's the state's star witness. Let's say the victim had lived in this case and it was an aggravated assault case. Why  wouldn't the state be able to talk to the witness overnight and coach their testimony if  -- if the -- if the --if the victim is not doing very well on the stand?  The same logic would apply the other way.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, I -- I think I might have missed the  --the answer. You  -- the -- the question was like, can you tell the witness to stop mumbling?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: You said he can't say that?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: No. I would consider  -- how you present yourself to the  --the jury, I consider that to be  also coaching, a form of coaching. Look him in the eye, stop mumbling, don't talk as quickly, things of that nature.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What's the exact formulation, if you have one, of what the judge should say to the counsel before an overnight recess?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Well, I would say something like this, if  -- for a managing order or testimony generally?  A managing order?  I would  --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: If I were the judge, I would say:  Okay, I'm not going to go through  an entire list of everything you can talk about, but whenever you're sitting there talking to your client, I want you to ask yourself, is this directly talking about the testimony or is this talking -- is -- conceptually, is this something that's a derivative matter from it, and, in any event, regardless of what you're talking about, does this require you to coach, regroup, or strategize about the testimony itself?

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Don't you think lawyers are going to have very different answers to that question when they ask themselves that question?  I guess the  point being, is that line really able to be applied in a neutral and equal manner?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: They  --they might have some differences of opinion, but we do trust lawyers to use their judgment whenever they are complying with any kind of court order. And our rule also allows any lawyer, if they're just not sure and they  -- they really don't want to run afoul of the court's order, to come back and ask the trial court and to say:  Your Honor, I wasn't sure about this, can I get some clarification or even maybe some reconsideration?  And let's say it's, like, 5 in the morning or something like that and they don't want to bother the trial court judge at 5 in the morning. They can wait to the morning and they can explain the problem that they were having and then they can ask for a continuance.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So that's -- that's important. That's your solution to ambiguity for the lawyers, is just come back and ask if there's something you want to talk about?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Counsel, let me ask you about the government's -- the -- the Solicitor General's position, which we've now established is much broader. It's a no-testimony order.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: What are your thoughts on the workability of that?  I mean, the  -- the -- the counsel on the other side suggests that there are all kinds of discussions that involve trial strategy that are going to be related to the defendant's testimony, so a no-testimony order might be sweeping too broadly, or at least that's the argument. Do you have an opinion about that?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: I think that it fits with the Court's case law. I do -- ours -- our rule  --their rule is a much more bright-line rule. So, in a way, it would be easier to comply with. But it's not as flexible. What our rule -- the -- the benefit of our rule is it maximizes the amount of conferral that an attorney could possibly have about the matters they do have a constitutional right to talk about. But, at the same time, it -- it excises as much as possible to protect the integrity of trial the things that they don't have the right to talk about, which is the ongoing  -- the substance of the ongoing testimony.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, Justice Gorsuch asked you the facts of this case are  really your position in this case, which is the judge's order only limited management. But the  SG wants us to announce a greater -- a bigger rule.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you agree with that rule?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Their rule would -- you're asking if I agree with it. I think it would be supported by the case law, yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. How would it be supported by logic?  Putting aside -- you agree that under any circumstance a lawyer has an obligation, even we've said it in Nix, to not suborn perjury.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If a client is suborning -- is perjuring him or herself  --

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: -- the lawyer just can't sit there and not do something about that.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So that's the close case here, what can they do.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You would say they can say to the  --to the defendant:  If you commit perjury, these are the consequences. Correct?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If the government says no, that's really dangerous. How about plea bargain?  The government said no in its brief to saying: Your testimony is really bad.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You should reconsider taking this. If the lawyer doesn't do that, he or she is not supporting their ethical obligation to give the defendant information, adequate information, to consider a plea, correct?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Well, I think, in order to comply with court orders, you are able to --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Excuse  the ethical obligation?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Well, to  --yes, to a certain extent. I  --I -- I would be surprised about that.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. But putting that aside, at what point do we accept the SG's position?  Because an order that says don't talk to the person at all we said in Geders -- in Perry was wrong. In -- I'm sorry -- in Geders, we said:  Don't talk to the defendant at all.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: That's correct. And that  --that  --that  --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the question is where to draw the line. Should we ignore that line here?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Well, I think, in Perry, they drew the line at -- between testimony and not testimony. I mean, they even go so far in Perry as to say:  You can tell them not to talk at all because we're so worried that yo u're going to go talk to them about their testimony.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Right. But we said  --we said in Geders you can't do it overnight.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Not  -- not -- they say over  --that was the context, although I would say they say a long break. But the problem with  -- as --as was brought up earlier, drawing the temporal line between the two is a problem.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Makes no sense, so I don't. I do between what you can talk about and what you can't.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yeah. Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Thank you.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Counsel  --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, I'm sorry, please.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: I want to just read you a potential instruction, and tell me if you agree with it or if you see any kind of difference between your position and what I'm going to say and what the SG says. What if the court says  --or what if we were to say that qualified conferral orders are okay if they tell the lawyer:  Listen, you  can't talk about the content of the testimony or the manner of its delivery, but you can discuss any strategic consequences of the defendant's testimony, such as whether to take a plea, whether to call another witness, et cetera?  Are you okay with that?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. And I'll ask the SG whether the SG thinks that's consistent too. Okay.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. Warthen, I just want to q uickly follow up on Justice Sotomayor's line of questioning. And I wonder whether unconstitutional conditions doctrine might be in play if you were to -- if you were to say that a lawyer couldn't advise a defendant during a long break about collateral consequences from his testimony, if I can't advise you, boy, it's time to take that plea  --

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: -- if I can't tell you about the consequences of perjury, you know, maybe  -- maybe I'm excused as an ethical matter because I'm under a court order perhaps, but I would have thought that our unconstitutional conditions doctrine would have  something to say about unnecessarily chilling the Sixth Amendment right that's at stake here.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Now are you asking from the no -management perspective or the SG's?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: The SG's perspective. I wonder whether that implicates unconstitutional conditions doctrine.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: I don't want to put words in her mouth, but I think the SG would say that there's two ways to go about dealing with, say, perjury. One, if you  --if they are still on direct, you can try to fix it up as you're going on direct, or if it happens on cross-examination, you can try to fix it up on redirect. Another option would be the defendant finishes his testimony, you tell the trial court:  Something very important came up during his testimony, if we could just have a moment to  -- to talk, if we can just have a little bit of time. Then, at that time, you talk to them, you  -- you investigate whether or not perjury happened, you remonstrate with them about your  ethical obligations and his obligations, and then you would call him back as a witness. And then the -- if the trial court  were to be, like, well, why are you calling him back as a witness, you could either say, it'll become clear in just a moment, Your Honor, or you ca n tell them -- you just have to tell them straight out, perjury was committed and I have to fix it before the end of this proceeding. One other thing I just want to -- since I have a little bit of time, it's just the rule that we're advancing, it does -- it is the  -- the rule that I believe supports federalism. It's the rule that  --what they're asking to do is put a rule that is virtually unalterable because it would require a constitutional amendment. And we very rarely micromanage trial courts in that manner with these -- with these kind of constitutional rules.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Let me ask you about your -- your perjury exception. So you  -- you say that the -- the defense counsel could advise the defendant when there's a break during cross-examination, an overnight break during cross-examination, to avoid perjury. Doesn't that -- would that allow defense counsel to help to clean up all inconsistencies between what the defendant said on direct and what the defendant admitted under cross?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: So could you phrase it one more time, Your Honor, just to make sure I understand?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah. On  --on direct, the defendant testifies the way it was  --it was anticipated before trial and says A.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: And then, on cross, the defendant messes up and says B, something that's completely inconsistent. Doesn't that allow the defense attorney to say:  Look, you know, you're under oath. You can't commit perjury.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Clean up what you said on cross-examination.

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: Mm -hmm.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: That would be allowed?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: I would say this. You would want to investigate with them to see if  there actually was perjury, and then you need to remonstrate with them, and then you would tell them:  Okay, tomorrow what we're going to do is we're going to clean this up. I'm not going to tell you how because I'm under court order, but just know  --be expecting that tomorrow this is going to be fixed. You don't need to tell them, okay, here's the best thing to say or, you know, like, what -- true or false, this is the best thing to say. I'm going to ask you these particular questions, so be expecting these questions. That's all strategizing. You couldn't do those things. But what you could do is just establish that perjury was committed and let the defendant know this is going to be dealt with tomorrow.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: And how about the same thing with the -- with the question of -- of plea bargaining?  Could defense counsel say,  wow, that was not good because you were mumbling, because you were scowling, you better correct all the -- you know, you better correct all that stuff?

turns.name: ANDREW N. WARTHEN
turns.text: I don't think that would be allowed. What  -- what we say in our brief is that you can tell them -- you could say you need to take the plea bargain. You could  -- you might need to take the plea bargain bec ause the state's case was so strong but also if he just did a terrible job. And if the defendant says, well, how did I do a terrible job, you would just have to tell them I'm under a court order, I cannot tell you why that's true, but you need to  -- in my professional opinion, you need to understand that the best course of action for you now is to take a plea bargain.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Now why -- why is that, Mr. Warthen?  I mean, if you had said, as you do on page 14, that you can talk to the defendant about trial testimony when it's incidental to a big trial strategy decision like whether to take a plea bargain, and th e person says to you, I don't understand, like, what do you think went wrong, like, why was it so serious that I now have to tell this?  And you say I can't tell you, just trust me that  you have to take a plea bargain. And the person says, what do you mean, trust me?
